subject: Cardiology
title: Aneurysm
preambleMarkdown:
# Aneurysm

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Aneurysm is a clinically important vascular topic in which the key question is whether blood flow, vessel-wall integrity, embolic risk, or end-organ perfusion is threatened. Exam questions usually test the emergency pattern, the first imaging test, when medical therapy is enough, and when vascular, neurologic, or interventional consultation is urgent.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess perfusion, neurologic status, and hemodynamic stability first.
- Smoking cessation and lipid management are central in atherosclerotic vascular disease.
- Duplex ultrasound is useful for many stable vascular diagnoses.
- CTA is useful when anatomy, dissection, rupture, or intervention planning matters.
- Urgent specialist involvement is required for threatened limb, stroke risk, rupture, or major bleeding.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Aneurysm is defined by the specific vessel abnormality in the topic: stenosis, occlusion, aneurysmal dilation, dissection, thrombosis, embolization, or chronic flow limitation. The diagnosis should name the vessel bed, acuity, symptoms, and complication risk.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- By vessel bed: aorta, carotid/vertebral circulation, peripheral arteries, or deep venous system.
- By mechanism: stenosis, occlusion, aneurysm, dissection, thrombosis, embolization, inflammation, or traumatic injury.
- By acuity: asymptomatic, chronic symptomatic, acute complicated, or limb-/organ-threatening.
- By treatment context: medical surveillance, antithrombotic therapy, endovascular repair, open repair, or emergency intervention.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Vascular disease risk depends on the vessel bed. Arterial disease clusters with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, kidney disease, age, and systemic atherosclerosis; aneurysm risk is strongly linked to age, male sex, smoking, family history, and connective-tissue disease; venous thrombosis follows stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Relevant anatomy is the involved vascular territory: aorta and branch vessels for aneurysm or dissection, carotid and vertebral circulation for neurovascular events, lower-extremity arterial segments for claudication or limb ischemia, and deep venous pathways for thrombosis. Anatomy determines symptoms, imaging choice, and intervention options.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Arterial disease reduces oxygen delivery through stenosis, occlusion, embolization, rupture, or false-lumen flow. Venous disease impairs return and can embolize to the lungs. Collateral circulation, perfusion pressure, clot burden, and end-organ reserve determine symptom severity.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Processes include atherosclerotic plaque formation, thrombosis, embolization, aneurysmal wall degeneration, intimal tearing with dissection, inflammatory vasculopathy, traumatic injury, and venous clot propagation. Complications depend on whether flow is blocked, tissue is ischemic, or a vessel ruptures.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Causes include atherosclerosis, smoking-related endothelial injury, diabetes, hypertension, connective-tissue disease, bicuspid aortic valve, trauma, catheter or procedural injury, inflammation, infection, malignancy, immobility, pregnancy, and inherited or acquired thrombophilia.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess smoking, diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, chronic kidney disease, age, family history, prior vascular disease, connective-tissue features, atrial fibrillation, cancer, immobility, estrogen exposure, pregnancy, trauma, and recent procedures.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pain, pulselessness, pallor, paresthesia, weakness, swelling, neurologic deficit, bruit, claudication, rest pain, ulceration, or shock depending on vascular bed.
- Some disease is found by screening or incidental imaging.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute limb ischemia.
- Transient ischemic attack or stroke symptoms.
- Severe tearing chest/back pain.
- Hypotension or syncope with aneurysm.
- Phlegmasia, pulmonary embolism, or major bleeding on anticoagulation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Consider musculoskeletal pain, neuropathy, cellulitis, venous insufficiency, lymphedema, ruptured Baker cyst, pulmonary embolism, stroke mimics, aortic dissection, acute coronary syndrome, renal colic, and gastrointestinal causes depending on the vascular bed.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use pulse examination, ankle-brachial index when relevant, duplex ultrasound, CTA/MRA, and labs directed by acuity.
- Assess embolic sources, thrombophilia, malignancy, or inflammatory disease when suggested.
- Do not delay emergency intervention for nonessential testing.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use bedside pulse and neurologic assessment, ABI when PAD is suspected, duplex ultrasound for carotid or venous disease, CTA/MRA for dissection, aneurysm anatomy, acute limb ischemia, or procedural planning, and labs targeted to anticoagulation, renal function, bleeding risk, infection, or inflammatory disease.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Stabilize emergencies and involve vascular, neurologic, or interventional teams early.
- Use antiplatelet, anticoagulant, thrombolytic, endovascular, or surgical therapy according to diagnosis.
- Treat risk factors aggressively.
- Provide surveillance after aneurysm repair, stenting, or bypass.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Six Ps suggest acute limb ischemia.
- Symptomatic carotid stenosis is managed differently from asymptomatic stenosis.
- DVT diagnosis combines pretest probability, D-dimer, and compression ultrasound.
- Aortic dissection management depends on type A versus type B.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI. 2025 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2025-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-acute-coronary-syndromes
- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2024 Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease. https://professional.heart.org/-/media/PHD-Files-2/Science-News/2/2024/2024-PAD-guideline-slide-set.pdf

### Source Excerpt: Aneurysmal Vascular Disease

_Source lines: 15946-16489._

```text
ANEURYSMAL VASCULAR DISEASE
I. General Principles
Important deHnitions/descriptions include:
A. Aneurysm (derived from the Greek word aneuisma) : a permanent localized enlarge
ment that is 1 . 5 times or greater than the diameter of the normal proximal artery
B. Arterial ectasia: a localized enlargement less than 1 . 5 times of normal diameter
C. Arteriomegaly: generalized arterial enlargement in contiguous vessels, less than
1 . 5 times normal, and a well-recognized risk factor for progression to aneurysmal
proportions
D. True aneurysm: enlargement composed of all three layers (tunics) of the vessel wall
E. False aneurysm: Also known as pseudoaneurysms , they lack at least one and
sometimes all three layers of the vessel wall. Blood is contained by perivascular
connective tissue and, if present, the tunica adventitia.

F . Aortic dissection: passage of blood from the arterial lumen into the arterial wall,
thereby creating a false lumen. The false lumen may reestablish flow back into
the main arterial lumen and become a double-barrel system.

G. Dissecting aneurysm: aortic dissection with aneurysmal dilatation of the false
lumen. These are prone to rupture.

H . Mycotic aneurysm: refers to any aneurysms that result from direct infectious
process resulting in destruction of the supportive tissue in the arterial wall.

These compose a small ( <5%) proportion of all aneurysms. Bacteria and fungi
are the most commonly implicated organisms.

I. Syphilitic aneurysm: Although extremely rare in this day and age , this is
characteristic of tertiary syphilis and occurs secondary to inflammatory disease
of the ascending thoracic aorta.

]. Infrarenal aneurysm: aneurysm with a normal-caliber aorta between the renal
arteries and the aneurysm
K. Juxtarenal aneurysm: involves the renal arteries or is within l em of the renal
arteries
II. Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms lAAAsl
A. Anatomy (Fig. 1 3-2) : The abdominal aorta is a direct continuation of the thoracic
aorta as it passes through the aortic hiatus in the diaphragm. It lies in the retro
peritoneum and slightly to the left of midline. It bifurcates into the right and left
common iliac arteries at the level of L4 .

l . The major branches include the celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, renal
artery, and the inferior mesenteric artery.

2. Other named vessels of clinical interest are the inferior phrenic, gonadal, and
lumbar arteries.

3. There are two main types of aneurysms: fusiform and saccular (Fig. 13-3 ) .

B. Epidemiology
l . AAAs affect about l % to 3% of the general population. Aging of the population
and increasing use of routine radiographic studies has resulted in an increase
in the prevalence of AAA.

V A S C U L A R S U R G E R Y e 2 2 1
2 . AAAs are the 1 3 th leading cause of death i n all age groups.

a. They are the third leading cause of sudden death in males older than
70 years of age.

b . Ruptured AAAs account for 1 5 ,000 deaths per year in the United States.

C . Risk factors for AAA (Table 13-5)
l. Smoking: This is by far the single most important environmental risk factor.

The prevalence of AAA in smokers is four times that of nonsmokers.

2. Age: Natural tissue degeneration and atherosclerosis increase with age and predis
pose to the formation of aneurysms. Most patients are 65 years of age and older.

3. Gender: Males are at least six times more likely to develop AAA than women.

Fam i ly h i story
4. Infections: Salmonellosis and tuberculosis have been implicated in the
formation of certain aneurysms.

5. Connective tissue disorders: Individuals with Marfan syndrome and Ehlers
Danlos type IV have an increased risk of aneurysm.

D . Risk factors for rupture of AAA-which patients need elective surgery (Table 13-6)
l. Size: the single most important factor. According to the Laplace law, the ten
sion on the wall of a fluid-containing structure is a product of the radius
and pressure (T = P X r) . Increasing size not only results in increasing wall
tension but also implies weakening of the aortic wall. Generally, aneurysms
5 . 5 em or larger should be repaired.

2. Hypertension: The second variable in the Laplace equation, hypertension
(uncontrolled) can result in rapid enlargement of the aneurysm and a higher
risk of rupture for any given size of aneurysm.

3. Smoking: Especially if there is associated chronic obstructive pulmonary
disease (COPD ) , smoking is an independent risk factor for rapid enlargement
and eventual rupture of AAA.

4. Gender: Although AAA are more common in males, size for size, females are
at three times the risk of rupture. The recent increasing popularity of smoking
among females is drawing the statistics closer between males and females.

5. Family history: There is a clear association here , as 25% of patients who
present with AAA rupture have a first-degree relative with a history of a
ruptured AAA.

E. Clinical features and diagnosis
l . Most AAA are asymptomatic and are discovered incidentally.

2. Symptoms can be due to mass effect and irritation of regional sensory nerves
(i. e . , back anor abdominal pain) or due to showering of clots distally
(thromboembolism) .

3. Abdominal sonography is a good screening and surveillance tool. In experienced
hands, the accuracy can reach margins of 3 mm.

4. CT scans with intravenous (IV) contrast and high resolution serve as confir
matory evidence and also help with surgical planning and approach.

F. Management (Table 13-7)
l. The decision to electively repair AAA is based on size and associated risk
factors. The surgeon needs to weigh risk of rupture versus risks and com
plications of surgery. In the absence of signiHcant risk factors for rupture ,
aneurysms smaller than 5 em can be followed by surveillance sonography or
CT scans .

TAB LE 1 3-7 Management of Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms
<5.5 em
Fo l l ow-up screen i n g with either abdom i n a l u ltrasound or with CT scan every 6 months.

5.5 em, presence of symptoms or rap i d ly expanding (>5 mm per 6 months)

2. Symptomatic aneurysm (i. e . , aneurysms that are tender to palpation) and those
that are rapidly enlarging (more than 5 mm/6 months) should be repaired
electively. As shown in Table 13-8, the annual risk of rupture significantly
increases with increasing size of the aneurysm.

3. In patients who warrant surgical treatment, there are three different
techniques: endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) , open transperitoneal, and
open retroperitoneal. Circulation of pressurized blood through the aneurysm
sac is responsible for expansion and eventual rupture of all aneurysms.

a. The choice of procedure is determined by several factors, most importantly
the anatomy of the aneurysm. When trying to decide on the most suitable
approach key factors to keep in mind, include previous history, including
surgical history; unusual anatomic features on the CT scan; and comorbid
conditions. One such memory tool is outlined in Figure 1 3-4.

b . The basic principle of all three techniques is to channel blood from the
normal-caliber aorta proximally to the normal-caliber aorta or iliacs distally,
thereby excluding the aneurysm from the circulation. This is achieved by
placing nonexpansile yet durable synthetic tubes (grafts) in the aorta at the
two ends of the aneurysm. In the open approach, the graft is sutured to the
aorta using a nonabsorbable suture. In EVAR, the graft is attached to the
aorta by metallic hooks.

G . Ruptured AAA
l . Most feared complication of AAA
2. Carries an overall mortality rate of more than 80%
Ruptured AAA. CT of (A) axial and ( B ) coron a l views.

T h e retro p e rito n e u m c a n
s o m etimes conta i n rupture

ureteral c o l i c .

## 3 . Presents with acute abdominal pain, back pain, and hypotension

4. Immediate operative intervention is the only treatment that can offer hope for
survival.

H. Inflammatory AAA
l . Inflammatory AAAs represent about 5% of all infrarenal AAAs.

2. There is usually a dense fibroinflammatory tissue that may be adherent to the
fourth portion of the duodenum, inferior vena cava, and left renal vein.

3. These aneurysms can also have associated ureteral involvement with renal
outflow obstruction.

4. Patients can present with pain, weight loss, and elevated erythrocyte
sedimentation rate .

## 5 . CT scan is diagnostic and shows four layers : aortic lumen, luminal clot

(thrombus) , thickened aortic wall, and adjacent inflammatory tissue .

6 . Inflammatory aneurysms are less prone t o rupture.

## 7 . This is a technical challenge , and most surgeons prefer either EVAR or the

open retroperitoneal approach.

Ill. Thoracic Aorta: Aneurysms and Dissections
A. Anatomy: Thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysms (TAAs) refer to fixed aortic dilatation,
starting anywhere from the descending thoracic aorta from the left subclavian to the
aortic bifurcation. The aneurysmal segments of the aorta may involve intercostal
arteries, celiac axis, superior and inferior mesenterica, renals, and gonadal vessels.

B. Classification: DeBakey (type I - III) and Stanford (A and B) (Table 13-9 and
Fig. 13-5)
C. Etiology: The majority of these aneurysms are due to degenerative changes
secondary to abnormal collagen and elastin metabolism. Previous chronic aortic
dissections are the second most common cause. Connective tissue disorders,
trauma, and vasculitis (especially Takayasu aortitis) make up a small percentage.

D. Clinical features: Most TAAs, like most AAAs, are discovered incidentally. When
symptomatic, TAAs can present with pain or signs of distal embolization. Mass effect
symptoms include dyspnea and dysphagia from compression of the aerodigestive
tract or ascites, visceromegaly, and lower extremity swelling from caval compression.

E. Management: Surgical repair, as with AAAs , comprises replacement of diseased
aortic segment by an artificial graft. The approach requires gaining exposure in
the thoracic as well as the abdominal cavities. The complex anatomy of TAA
makes their repair a surgical challenge .

l . Indications for repair include the presence of symptoms that are attributable to
the aneurysm. In the absence of symptoms, size greater than 6 em or increase
in size of more than 0 . 5 em per 6-month interval are accepted criteria for
elective repair.

2. To the operating team, perfusion of the spinal cord and abdominal viscera
during the aortic clamping is of paramount importance. Cerebral spinal fluid
( CSF) drainage is necessary to maintain perfusion of spinal cord. This is best
achieved by a specialized team approach.

TAB LE 1 3-9 Classi6cation of Thoracoabdominal Aortic Aneurysms
DeBakey Description Percentage
Type I Ascending and descend i n g aorta ( most common) 60%
Type I I Ascending only 1 0%-1 5%

3. Like AAAs , TAAs are also amenable to thoracic endovascular aortic repair
(TEVAR) . TEVAR and fenestrated grafts have demonstrated excellent
results for treatment of such lesions in patients with suitable or modifiable
anatomy. If the subclavian artery is covered by the endograft, the patient may
require revascularization of the left subclavian if the patient has a patent left
internal mammary artery coronary bypass, the left vertebral artery terminates
in the posterior inferior cerebellar artery, an occluded right vertebral, or
planned extensive coverage of more than 20 em of aorta. Open repair and
debranching can be undertaken but will result in higher morbidity and
mortality.

4. Thoracic aorta injury: TEVAR is considered first-line treatment for thoracic
aorta transection (Fig. 1 3-6) .

5 . Thoracic aorta dissections: Type A aortic dissections are a surgical emergency.

Medical management with blood pressure control is still the standard of care
for type B dissections. Invasive intervention is reserved for failure of medical
management or the presence of a complication such as malperfusion or
rupture.

IV. Femoral and Popliteal Aneurysms
A. Epidemiology and etiology
l . Almost 90% of the peripheral aneurysms are found in the femoral and
popliteal arteries.

2 . Popliteal aneurysms alone make up about 70% of the peripheral aneurysms.

The majority are secondary to degenerative atherosclerotic disease and are
predominantly an affliction of men.

B. Clinical features: These aneurysms, although not life threatening, can certainly be
limb threatening in a number of ways. Accumulation of thrombus , distal propa
gation, and limb-threatening ischemia can often be the presenting catastrophic
features. Other symptoms include pain secondary to nerve compression or edema
and venous thrombosis secondary to venous outflow obstruction.

C. Diagnosis
l . Due to its deep location, the popliteal artery is barely palpable in normal
individuals. Prominent pulsation in the popliteal fossa is an indication that the
patient may be harboring a popliteal aneurysm. Finding an aneurysm in these
vessels should prompt a thorough evaluation because 50% are bilateral and
30% have an aortic aneurysm associated with them.

## 2 . If suspected by physical examination, duplex ultrasonography can be used

to conHrm the anatomic conHguration as well as the flow dynamics of the
aneurysmal segment.

D. Management: Indications for intervention include thromboembolic complications
and size. It is generally agreed that femoral aneurysms of greater than 2 . 5 em
and popliteal aneurysms of greater than 2 . 0 em should be repaired. Here, unlike
AAAs, replacement with artiHcial graft is generally unnecessary, and usage of
autogenous vein is feasible. Endovascular treatment with placement of covered
stents that can exclude the aneurysm from the circulation is also a popular
approach.

V. Splenic Artery Aneurysms
A. Splenic artery aneurysms (SAAs) are the most common of the visceral artery
aneurysms. After the aorta and iliacs, they are the third most common
intra-abdominal aneurysms.

B. Unlike most aneurysms, SAAs are predominantly a disease of the female sex.

V A S C U L A R S U R G E R Y e 2 2 7
Moreover, there is a close association with parity. More than 90% females with
SAAs have been pregnant at least once.

C . Like most aneurysms, SAAs are asymptomatic .

D . Indications for treatment are presence of symptoms , documented enlargement,
pregnancy or anticipated pregnancy, and diameter of greater than 2 . 5 em. SAA
discovered during pregnancy should be repaired because pregnancy greatly
increases the risk of rupture.

E . All potential surgical candidates should receive preoperative immunizations,
similar to splenectomy patients (i. e . , Haemophilus, Pneumococcus, Meningococcus,
influenza) . However, aneurysm repair with splenic preservation is the ideal
treatment.

F. The main complication of SAA is rupture.

VI. Renal Artery Aneurysms
A. Aneurysms of the renal artery are the second most common visceral aneurysm.

Even so, they occur in about 0 . 09% of the general population. They are more
commonly seen in the female multiparous population.

B. They, like other aneurysms, are discovered as incidental lesions. Most are
associated with hypertension.

C . Indications for surgery

## 1 . Difficult-to-control hypertension

2. Size of greater than 2 em
3. In the majority of cases, excellent response with blood pressure control is seen.

VII. Mycotic Aneurysms
A. Mycotic aneurysms result from localized infection, which may be a consequence
of periaortic infectious process, or due to aortic intimal seeding Xom bloodborne
pathogens . The most common infectious bacteria are Staphylococcus aureus
and Salmonella typhi, and the most common fungal agents are Candida albicans
and Aspergillus Lmigatus. Bacterial infections are more common than fungal
infections. The usual sites are the femoral arteries and the aorta.

B. Clinical presentation can be nonspecific but with recurrent bacteremia or
fungemia. Fevers, chills, and tenderness not attributable to size alone should
prompt the physician to include an infected aneurysm in the differential
diagnosis .

C. Management is resection and repair of the aneurysm, thorough debridement of
infected tissue, and lifelong antibiotic treatment.

VIII. Pseudoaneurysms
A. Pseudoaneurysms (PSAs) are contained arterial disruptions. The main types are
traumatic and postsurgical.

## 1 . Traumatic PSAs are usually iatrogenic and are a result of arterial punctures for

various procedures. Because the femoral artery is the most common site for
such procedures , iatrogenic PSAs are predominantly found in this area.

2. Postsurgical PSAs are due to contained suture line disruptions between the
arterial wall and graft material.

B. The diagnosis can be made readily by duplex sonography.

C. Traumatic PSAs greater than 2 . 5 em are treated by inj ection of thrombin, a
procoagulant, in the cavity of the PSA under sonographic guidance. Those that
do not respond to this approach require open surgery to repair the localized
disruption in the vessel wall.

1 . Asymptomatic, traumatic PSAs less than 2 . 5 em do not require treatment.

2. Postsurgical PSAs most often require revision of the surgical site suture line.
```
